name: Apache ActiveMQ Message Broker
what_it_does: Open-source enterprise message broker with REST and protocol APIs — enabling Java and multi-language applications to implement JMS (Java Message Service) messaging, AMQP, STOMP, MQTT, and OpenWire protocols, with message persistence, transactions, clustering, and web console, used extensively in enterprise Java applications, Spring Boot, and as the foundation for Amazon MQ.
use_cases: Implementing JMS messaging via ActiveMQ for enterprise Java application integration
Building Spring Boot async messaging via ActiveMQ for microservice event-driven architectures
Using Amazon MQ (managed ActiveMQ) for AWS-hosted enterprise messaging without ops burden
AMQP/STOMP/MQTT protocol bridging via ActiveMQ for multi-protocol enterprise integration
Migrating legacy enterprise messaging (TIBCO, IBM MQ) to open-source via ActiveMQ
not_for: High-throughput stream processing at Kafka scale (ActiveMQ is queue-centric, not log-centric)
Teams without Java/JMS expertise for optimal configuration and tuning
Cloud-native microservices needing lightweight messaging (use NATS or RabbitMQ instead)
best_when: An enterprise Java team needs JMS-compatible messaging with full AMQP/STOMP support — or when using Amazon MQ for AWS-managed ActiveMQ without operational overhead.
avoid_when: You need Kafka-scale throughput, are building cloud-native non-Java microservices, or need the simplest possible message broker.

auth.notes: HTTP Basic auth for REST API (username/password). JMS/AMQP clients use username/password credentials. Default credentials: admin/admin (MUST be changed in production). JAAS authentication plugin for custom auth backends (LDAP, JDBC). Authorization via authorization policies in activemq.xml. No OAuth2. No webhooks — event-driven via JMS listeners. Amazon MQ: AWS IAM for broker access; standard ActiveMQ auth for messaging.

pricing.paid_tiers: Amazon MQ: ~$0.035/hour per broker (AWS pricing); RedHat AMQ (enterprise support)
pricing.notes: Apache Software Foundation project. Apache 2.0 license. 20+ years of history (since 2004). Two versions: Classic 5.x (original) and Artemis 2.x (next-gen, higher performance). Amazon MQ is managed ActiveMQ/RabbitMQ on AWS. Red Hat AMQ is enterprise-supported version (RHEL subscription). JMS spec compliance is key differentiator. Spring Integration / Spring Boot excellent support. Used by: Fortune 500 enterprises, government systems, healthcare, banking. STOMP for non-Java clients. MQTT for IoT. Persistent messages via KahaDB or JDBC. Transaction support (XA distributed transactions). Master-slave or replica clustering.

performance.rate_limits: Self-hosted — bounded by hardware; ~10K-50K msg/sec typical

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: REST API for queue management. Primary interaction via JMS/AMQP protocol SDK. HTTP Basic auth. Java-first but multi-protocol. Self-hosted. Apache 2.0. Default admin/admin credentials MUST be changed. Amazon MQ for managed deployment.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Persistent messages: at-least-once delivery — consumers must be idempotent. JMS message ID for deduplication. XA transactions for exactly-once with supporting XA resource managers. Idempotent consumer pattern required.
known_agent_gotchas: DEFAULT CREDENTIALS: admin/admin — MUST be changed before production deployment
Two ActiveMQ versions: Classic 5.x vs Artemis 2.x — different config, different performance profile
REST API is management-only — messaging via JMS/AMQP protocol, not HTTP
JMS is Java-centric — non-Java clients use STOMP, AMQP, or MQTT adapters
Artemis 2.x is recommended for new deployments — Classic 5.x is maintenance mode
Amazon MQ: ActiveMQ-compatible managed service — use for AWS deployments without ops burden

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS optional (must configure SSL in activemq.xml). Apache 2.0 open source — auditable. Default credentials admin/admin are HIGH RISK if not changed. JAAS auth for LDAP/JDBC integration. Authorization policies for queue/topic access control. No OAuth2. User-controlled data residency. Amazon MQ: AWS security posture (TLS enforced, IAM auth).
